score_relevant_coverage_miss.adjudicated_status: emitted discharge appeal/investigation record is directly supported
score_relevant_coverage_miss.reason: Agent B cites discharge grievance access, immediate fourth-step routing, arbitration access, and union notice. This is a real due-process object. Agent A's earlier missing ledger file made this a silent miss; after repair, the substantive adjudication favors the emitted record.

score_relevant_coverage_miss.adjudicated_status: emitted investigation/appeal package is supported by Article 16
score_relevant_coverage_miss.reason: Agent B captured private discussion rules, review before suspension/discharge, and record-removal protections. These are real due-process terms. Agent A did not emit the object, so this is a substantive miss by the non-emitting output.

score_relevant_coverage_miss.adjudicated_status: progressive discipline is supported, with CCA limitation
score_relevant_coverage_miss.reason: Agent B identifies letter-of-warning and short-suspension rules, plus an Appendix B limitation for CCAs. The object is not perfect, but it is real and scoreable with flags. Agent A's omission is a true miss.

score_relevant_coverage_miss.adjudicated_status: vacation/annual leave record is directly supported
score_relevant_coverage_miss.reason: Agent B captured choice-vacation period rules, annual-leave program continuation, and CCA accrual/payout terms. Agent A's notes acknowledge vacation was preserved but no concept record was emitted, so this is a true non-emitter miss.

scoreability_or_scalar_inclusion_boundar.preferred_treatment: record as external/contribution proxy, not active-medical generosity score
scoreability_or_scalar_inclusion_boundar.reason: Both agents identify hourly health/welfare fund contributions. The disagreement is whether those contributions are enough to score active medical contribution. Because the terms are fund/trust contributions and worker-facing premium/cost-sharing terms are absent, the safer treatment is to withhold the scalar and keep a proxy/profile record.

scoreability_or_scalar_inclusion_boundar.preferred_treatment: record holiday list and holiday-work premium, but do not score paid-holiday generosity without nonwork-day pay evidence
scoreability_or_scalar_inclusion_boundar.reason: Both agents find the holiday list and double-time holiday-work premium. The missing piece is ordinary paid holiday entitlement for nonwork days. Treating the holiday object as record-only is more defensible than scoring it as a paid holiday package.

scoreability_or_scalar_inclusion_boundar.preferred_treatment: score the narrow family-care leave increment, while keeping maternity/parental statutory reference as record-only
scoreability_or_scalar_inclusion_boundar.reason: Agent A captured only the statutory maternity/parental reference; Agent B also captured a CBA-contained family-care leave increment of up to three days with discretionary pay. The defensible fix is concept/subobject clarity: statutory parental leave is not scored, but the narrow family-care increment can receive a low flagged score.

scoreability_or_scalar_inclusion_boundar.preferred_treatment: score the CBA-contained SUB-linked premium/continuation rule with externality flags
scoreability_or_scalar_inclusion_boundar.reason: Both agents identify the same Article XXII continuation/premium-waiver rule. Although SPDs govern benefit design, the CBA-contained rule that benefits continue and health premiums are waived while receiving SUB is a worker-facing continuation term. Keep a moderate flagged score rather than treating it as pure external profile.

score_level_calibration.anchor_lesson: A reporting-pay rule can reach 4 when it combines a clear reporting floor with a larger minimum once work begins and a defined emergency/call-back variant.
A 10 percent shift differential with schedule-specific limits is ordinary rather than strong unless coverage or related protections are unusually broad.
Protected steward time, worksite access, anti-discrimination, and steward layoff/recall protections can justify 4 even when access is conditioned on non-interference.
Neutral and final arbitration remains a 3 when access is tightly step-gated and remedial authority is materially limited.
A complete grievance ladder should stay at 3 when strict waiver rules or procedural traps materially constrain access.
A narrow working-spouse reimbursement rule is scoreable but weak when it depends on a spouse plan rather than a broad active-medical contribution schedule.
